retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Although there is little evidence to support this, it has been postulated that alterations in the composition of the gut microbiota and defects in mucosal immunity could lead to ulcerative colitis. Autoimmunity may also play an important role in the etiology of ulcerative colitis. [4] [5] Some evidence suggests that smoking may be protective, but so far, no one has been able to confirm a direct relationship between the two.

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is also an association of inflammatory bowel disease with the removal of an inflamed appendix. Appendectomy before the age of twenty is associated with a decreased incidence of ulcerative colitis, whereas the opposite is true for Crohn disease. In fact, appendectomy has been shown to reduce the risk of developing ulcerative colitis by 69%. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Colitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary to immune deficiency disorders

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ulcerative colitis has a bimodal pattern of incidence. The main onset peaks between the age of 15 and 30 years. A second, and the smaller peak of incidence occurs between the age of 50 and 70 years. Though some studies show a slight predilection for men, most studies note no preference regarding sex. There is an increased prevalence of ulcerative colitis in nonsmokers or those who recently quit smoking. Additionally, smokers diagnosed with ulcerative colitis tend to have milder disease, fewer hospitalizations and need for less medication. There is evidence, though weak, that non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug use is associated with the onset or relapse of ulcerative colitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Worldwide, the highest incidence and prevalence of inflammatory bowel diseases are seen in Northern Europe and North America. Inflammatory bowel disease is closely linked to a Westernized environment and lifestyle. Ulcerative colitis has an incidence of 9 to 20 cases per 100,000 persons per year. Its prevalence is 156 to 291 cases per 100,000 persons per year. Compared to Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis has a greater prevalence in adults. When considering the pediatric population, however, ulcerative colitis is less prevalent than Crohn disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several other diseases with autoimmune backgrounds have correlations with microscopic colitis. Like other aspects, it appears that concomitant autoimmune diseases are more common in patients with collagenous colitis when compared to lymphocytic colitis. [15] [16] There have also been rare cases of concurrent microscopic colitis and inflammatory bowel disease, usually ulcerative colitis. [17] There have even been case reports of concurrent lymphocytic and collagenous colitis. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain the history and physical examination of patients with ulcerative colitis. Outline evaluation of patients with ulcerative colitis. Describe treatment considerations for patients with ulcerative colitis. Identify interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes in patients with ulcerative colitis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation will usually reveal an increase in inflammatory factors (ESR, CRP, leukocytosis), especially during an acute flare. Regardless of disease stage, 60% to 70% of ulcerative colitis patients are positive for perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (P-ANCA). P-ANCA is also found in a small number of patients with Crohn disease. In addition to P-ANCA, anti-saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies (ASCA) are found in both Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis but are more prevalent in Crohn disease; therefore, testing for both P-ANCA and ASCA has some utility in distinguishing types. Testing for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) can also be helpful in ulcerative colitis, as higher levels can indicate a flare.

retrieved_docs.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease is slightly more common in females compared to males, but ulcerative colitis appears to be equally present in both genders. IBD is generally a disorder in developed countries and colder climates.

retrieved_docs.title: Crohn Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses to consider when assessing patients with possible Crohn disease are listed below. Documentation of travel and exposure history and judicious use of laboratory, imaging, and histologic tests help differentiate the above diagnoses. Infectious enteritis and terminal ileitis Coccidioides Histoplasma Salmonella Tuberculosis Yersinia Infectious colitis Amebiasis Campylobacter C.difficile Cytomegalovirus E.coli Salmonella Shigella Noninfectious Behcet disease Common variable immunodeficiency Diverticulitis Drug-induced colitis, eg, from nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and immunotherapy Ischemic colitis Sarcoidosis Segmental colitis associated with diverticulosis Small vessel vasculitis Solitary rectal ulcer syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Collagenous and Lymphocytic Colitis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Histologic changes define the 2 types of microscopic colitis. This presentation of symptoms often leads to evaluation for other types of inflammatory bowel disease, including Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. The incidence has been increasing throughout northern Europe and northern North America, which is more common in women. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ulcerative Colitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Ulcerative colitis is a systemic disorder with no cure. The disorder has numerous extraintestinal involvement in addition to the colon. Thus, it is best managed by an interprofessional team. All patients with the disorder need lifelong monitoring. Because of the risk of colorectal cancer, surveillance colonoscopy should occur every 1-2 years. Further, since patients are often treated with biological agents, they need to undergo screening for melanoma and nonmelanoma skin cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Autoimmunity -- Clinical Significance -- Organ-Based Autoimmune Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Crohn disease: Inflammatory bowel disease characterized by patchy, transmural lesions that can affect the entire gastrointestinal tract, with the possibility of fistulas, erythema nodosum, and pyoderma gangrenosum [16] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Extraintestinal Manifestations of Inflammatory Bowel Disease -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: EIMs are seen in 5% to 50% of the IBD patients during their lifetime. [1] EIMS occurs in 31% of patients suffering from Crohn disease and 43% of patients with ulcerative colitis and is more prevalent among females (50%) than males (34%). The frequency of EIMs overall is lower among the pediatric population compared with the adults. During a lifetime, one EIM is seen in 63% of the patients, whereas two EIM are reported in 27% of the IBD patients. Among all the EIMs, arthritis is the most common, followed by oral aphthous ulcers and uveitis. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2327
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)) includes a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is classified as an autoimmune disease, as is non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) and acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب). Inflammatory joint disease (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also considered members of this autoimmune group. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying autoimmune mechanism guides clinicians toward appropriate immunomodulatory treatments and vigilant monitoring for systemic involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2475
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية” includes several distinct disorders: التهاب المفاصل, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية, and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية. This grouping reflects that both autoimmune and non‑autoimmune forms of hemolytic anemia, as well as inflammatory arthritis, are considered part of the broader spectrum of immune‑mediated diseases. Recognizing these conditions as members of the autoimmune disorder SMQ helps clinicians categorize and manage diseases that involve dysregulated immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course. This chronic course can evolve into secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and also into chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). In addition, glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) is clinically associated with chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). These interconnections indicate that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease are at risk for metabolic complications such as secondary diabetes and subsequent renal impairment, and that glomerulonephritis may further contribute to chronic kidney dysfunction. Recognizing and monitoring these linked conditions are essential for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: how specific autoimmune and hematologic diseases fit within broader disease families, identify pharmacologic options and contraindications for their management, connect advanced nuclear‑medicine diagnostic modalities, and clarify the hierarchical organization of symptoms and immune‑related disorders for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 969
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, a pattern that is also observed in secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease and in chronic kidney failure (قصور كلوي مزمن). Necrotizing glomerulonephritis represents a specific, severe form of glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) and is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), an autoimmune condition that manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis is frequently associated with pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), reflecting its systemic impact. Glomerulonephritis, in turn, is clinically associated with the development of chronic kidney failure, underscoring the progressive nature of renal injury in these disorders. Together, these relationships highlight how chronic inflammatory processes, autoimmune kidney diseases, and metabolic complications interrelate and contribute to long‑term renal impairment, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 146
retrieved_docs.content: Gastroduodenal ulcer (excluding hemorrhage) and duodenal ulcer are subcategories of intestinal ulceration, which is linked to the bleeding terminology SMQ. Intestinal functional disorder is placed under both intestinal ulceration and intestinal spasm. Diverticulosis and diverticulitis are classified as forms of intestinal spasm, reflecting their relationship to motility disturbances. Peptic inflammation is a subordinate condition of gastritis and duodenitis. Other circulatory disease is situated within the broader category of connective‑tissue disorders and shows a clinical association with systemic lupus erythematosus, highlighting the interplay between vascular pathology and autoimmune connective‑tissue disease. Collectively, these classifications and associations illustrate how dermatologic, hematologic, gastrointestinal, circulatory, and autoimmune entities are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding disease mechanisms and guiding patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: range of autoimmune hematologic and rheumatologic disorders are interconnected within the broader context of circulatory and connective‑tissue diseases, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1332
retrieved_docs.content: Arthritis, acquired hemolytic anemia, autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia, non‑immune hemolytic anemia, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions (SMQ). This grouping highlights that arthritis can arise from autoimmune mechanisms, while hemolytic anemias may be either driven by autoantibodies (autoimmune hemolytic anemia) or occur through other, non‑immune pathways (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Coronary artery inflammation is likewise recognized as an autoimmune manifestation affecting the vascular system. Understanding that these diverse diseases share a common autoimmune framework aids clinicians in recognizing overlapping pathogenic processes and in applying immunomodulatory strategies where appropriate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: these relationships illustrate how various deficiency and hemolytic anemias are organized under a common classification, how specific anemias relate to ocular complications, and how many of these conditions, along with related inflammatory diseases such as arthritis and coronary artery inflammation, are considered part of the broader spectrum of immune‑mediated disorders. This integrated view supports comprehensive diagnostic reasoning and coordinated patient management across hematologic, ophthalmologic, and autoimmune domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: روماتزمي، غير ذلك وغير محدد. Chronicity also characterizes secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, and chronic inflammatory small bowel disease, indicating that prolonged inflammation or metabolic dysfunction underlies persistent pathology in the cardiovascular, renal, hepatic, endocrine, and gastrointestinal domains. Uncontrolled disease states are reflected in the General clinical state and are closely tied to diabetes mellitus with complications. Together, these relationships highlight how acute rheumatic inflammation, functional failure, chronic disease progression, specific anatomic sites, and multilingual terminology interconnect to inform comprehensive patient evaluation, monitoring, and treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.
